authoritySource: Dr. Ahmed Hesham — OET Pronunciation Rulebook for Healthcare Professionals (Medicine) v1

rules.title: /θ/ voiceless th as in 'think'
rules.body: Tongue tip lightly between or behind upper teeth; breath flows without voicing. Do NOT substitute /t/, /s/, or /f/. Medical vocabulary: therapy, thyroid, thrombosis, anaesthetic, asthma, method, author.
exemplarPhrases: The patient was prescribed thyroid replacement therapy.

rules.title: /ð/ voiced th as in 'this'
rules.body: Same tongue position as /θ/ but with voicing. Substituting /d/ or /z/ is the most common L1 error. Medical vocabulary: this, that, rhythm, breathe, either, smoothly.
exemplarPhrases: Please breathe smoothly; we'll listen to the rhythm.

rules.title: /v/ voiced labiodental as in 'vital'
rules.body: Upper teeth contact lower lip; voiced friction. Do NOT substitute /w/ or /f/. Medical vocabulary: vital, valve, vein, intravenous, invasive, ventilation, evaluate.

rules.title: /w/ voiceless glide as in 'wound'
rules.body: Lips round, no teeth contact. Distinct from /v/. Medical vocabulary: wound, ward, weight, swallow, word, warm.

rules.title: /r/ and /l/ contrast
rules.body: /r/ is an approximant — tongue tip curls toward the palate but does NOT tap. /l/ is a lateral — tongue tip touches the alveolar ridge. Never substitute. Medical vocabulary: rash/lash, rate/late, right/light, referred/labored.

rules.title: /p/ /t/ /k/ aspiration
rules.body: Voiceless stops in initial position are aspirated — a puff of air follows the release. Unaspirated stops sound like /b/ /d/ /g/ to English listeners. Particularly important in drug names: penicillin, paracetamol, tramadol.

params.articulation: aspirated stops when word-initial or stressed-syllable-initial

rules.title: Final consonants must not be dropped
rules.body: Medical vocabulary is dense with word-final consonants that carry meaning. Dropping them creates ambiguity: 'heart' vs 'hear', 'arrest' vs 'arrived'. Chinese and Vietnamese learners especially drop final /t/ /d/ /s/ /z/.

rules.title: /ŋ/ as in 'lung'
rules.body: Velar nasal. Distinct from /n/ + /g/. Medical vocabulary: lung, ringing, coughing, swelling, breathing.

rules.title: Short /ɪ/ as in 'symptom'
rules.body: Short, lax, front vowel. Do NOT replace with long /iː/. Symptom ≠ 'seem-tom'. Medical vocabulary: symptom, physical, clinic, insulin, rigid, dizzy, limit.

rules.title: Long /iː/ as in 'fever'
rules.body: Long, tense, high front. Medical vocabulary: fever, severe, anaemia, paediatric, seizure, relieve.

rules.title: TRAP vowel /æ/ as in 'catheter'
rules.body: Low front vowel. Distinct from /ʌ/ and /ɛ/. Medical vocabulary: catheter, analgesia, abdominal, fracture, allergy, cannula, back, cancer.

rules.title: NURSE vowel /ɜː/ as in 'nurse'
rules.body: Central mid, long. BrE/AusE non-rhotic; AmE rhotic. Medical vocabulary: nurse, word, alert, observed, burn, worse, hurt, emergency.

rules.title: STRUT vowel /ʌ/ as in 'blood'
rules.body: Low-mid central unrounded. Medical vocabulary: blood, gut, lung, onset, suffer, numb.

rules.title: Schwa /ə/ in unstressed syllables
rules.body: The schwa is the most frequent English vowel. Unstressed syllables in long medical words MUST reduce to schwa — failing to reduce sounds unnatural and harms intelligibility. Examples: doctor /ˈdɒktə/, hospital /ˈhɒspɪtəl/, patient /ˈpeɪʃənt/.

rules.title: FOOT vs GOOSE /ʊ/ vs /uː/
rules.body: Short lax /ʊ/ (good, could, put) vs long tense /uː/ (food, mood, tube, mucus). Medical: mucus, cough-syrup, pull, root-canal.

rules.title: /spl/ /spr/ /str/ /skr/ initial clusters
rules.body: Medical vocabulary is dense with three-consonant onsets. Insert no vowel between consonants. Key words: stroke, strain, spleen, splint, splash, stress, screen, script, strict.

rules.title: /kt/ /pt/ /kst/ final clusters
rules.body: Word-final clusters in OET-high-frequency vocabulary: infect, impact, script, concept, prompt, text, context.

rules.title: /nt/ /nd/ /ns/ /nz/ nasal clusters
rules.body: Common in past-tense medical verbs: examined, assessed, consulted, wanted, scanned. Do NOT insert a vowel: /ɪgˈzæmɪnd/ not /ɪgˈzæmɪnɪd/ (unless the verb stem ends in /t/ or /d/).

rules.title: Antepenultimate stress in -ology, -ologist, -ography
rules.body: Words ending -ology, -ologist, -ography take stress on the antepenultimate syllable: paTHOLogy, carDIology, raDIology, epidemiOLogy.

params.stressPattern: σ σ σ ˈ σ σ

rules.title: Penultimate stress in -tion, -sion, -cian
rules.body: Words ending -tion, -sion, -cian, -cious take stress on the syllable IMMEDIATELY before the suffix: examiNAtion, interVENtion, phySIcian, suSPIcion.

rules.title: Penultimate stress in -ic, -ical, -ity
rules.body: Words ending -ic, -ical, -ity take stress on the syllable immediately before the suffix: specIFic, clinICal, seveRIty, morbIDity.

rules.title: First-syllable stress in many noun drugs
rules.body: Many generic drug names take first-syllable stress: PAracetamol, IBuprofen, MORphine, INsulin, AMoxicillin (though amoxicillin is penultimate). When in doubt, check a dictionary — never guess.

rules.title: Noun vs verb stress shift
rules.body: Some medical words shift stress between noun and verb forms: REcord (n) vs reCORD (v); CONduct (n) vs conDUCT (v); INcrease (n) vs inCREASE (v).

rules.title: Content words are stressed, function words are reduced
rules.body: English is stress-timed: content words (nouns, main verbs, adjectives, adverbs) are stressed; function words (articles, prepositions, auxiliaries, pronouns) reduce to schwa. Failure to reduce sounds machine-like and reduces intelligibility.

rules.title: Pauses at clause boundaries, not mid-clause
rules.body: Natural pausing aids intelligibility. Pause at comma/full-stop positions or clause boundaries. Mid-clause hesitation (filled or empty) reduces fluency scoring.

rules.title: Rhythm: stressed syllables ~equidistant
rules.body: Stress-timed rhythm targets roughly equal intervals between stressed syllables, regardless of intervening unstressed syllables.

rules.title: Yes/no questions = rising intonation
rules.body: Questions answerable with yes/no take a rising intonation on the final stressed syllable. Critical for taking patient history: 'Do you smoke?', 'Are you allergic?', 'Have you had this before?'

rules.title: Wh-questions = falling intonation
rules.body: Questions starting with what/when/where/why/how/which take a falling intonation. 'WHAT brings you in today↘', 'WHERE is the pain↘'.

rules.title: Statements and instructions = falling intonation
rules.body: Clinical statements and instructions require a decisive falling tone: 'You should take this twice a day↘', 'We will admit you for observation↘'. Rising on statements sounds tentative and undermines clinical authority.

rules.title: Reassurance intonation — gentle rise-fall
rules.body: Reassuring a patient uses a soft rise-fall: 'This is going to be completely fine↗↘'. Monotone reassurance reads as insincere.

rules.title: Listing intonation — rise, rise, rise, fall
rules.body: When listing symptoms, investigations, or medications, each item rises until the last, which falls: 'We will order an ECG↗, a chest X-ray↗, and bloods↘.'

rules.title: Linking: final consonant to initial vowel
rules.body: In connected speech, a word-final consonant links to the next word's initial vowel: 'take it' → /teɪ kɪt/, 'not at all' → /nɒ tə tɔːl/. Staccato word-by-word speech reduces intelligibility.

rules.title: Weak forms of auxiliaries
rules.body: 'are /ə/', 'have /həv/', 'for /fə/', 'to /tə/', 'of /əv/', 'can /kən/'. Strong forms only under contrastive stress. 'I /ə/ referring you to...' not 'I /ɑːr/ referring you to...'.

rules.title: Contraction in speech only, not in writing
rules.body: Speak with contractions (I'm, you're, we'll) for naturalness, but never write them in OET letters (per grammar rule G07.1). Two different registers.

rules.title: Latin/Greek origin words — medical Latin stress
rules.body: Medical words of Latin origin preserve their stress: arthriTIS, bronCHItis, carDIac, epiDERmis. When the root shortens, stress shifts (arTHRItic).

rules.title: Eponymous conditions keep original phonetics
rules.body: Parkinson's, Crohn's, Alzheimer's — pronounce as the eponymous name.
